content.text: Every Python program, from a simple script to a full agentic pipeline, rests on two primitives: names that refer to objects, and objects that carry typed values. A precise mental model of how variables bind and how the type system is organized will prevent entire categories of bugs.
is not a box that holds a value — it is a name that refers to an object. When writing
the interpreter creates an integer object with the value 42 in memory, then binds the name
to that object in the current namespace. The object has three components: its

is dynamic and strong. Dynamic means a name can be rebound to an object of a different type at any point; strong means the interpreter refuses implicit coercions between incompatible types — adding a string and an integer raises
rather than silently converting one side.
Every value in Python is an instance of some class. Even
are singleton objects of their respective types. The built-in type hierarchy branches from a single root:
Understanding the variable and type model is prerequisite knowledge for almost everything else in Python. Incorrect assumptions cause concrete failures:
: passing a list into a function and modifying it in-place changes the caller's data too, because both names refer to the same object.
for large ones — testing identity instead of equality.
: an LLM API returns a string like
; feeding it directly into arithmetic raises
: assigning one list to another variable creates a second reference, not a second list.
: a function that returns nothing implicitly returns
; callers that chain attribute access on the result crash.
In agentic AI work, tool results and API responses are almost always strings or JSON-decoded structures (
). Getting the type conversion right before passing data between agents or into downstream computations is a core practical skill.
: no type declarations; code is concise and closely matches pseudocode descriptions of algorithms.
: a function can accept any type that supports the required operations (duck typing), reducing boilerplate.
cover most data-structure needs without importing anything.
let code inspect objects at runtime, which is invaluable during debugging.
add static checking where needed without rewriting everything.
: mistakes that a compiled language would catch at build time surface only when the code path executes.
: every operation involves type dispatch and reference counting. Numeric-heavy code is typically 10–100x slower than equivalent C.
: using a mutable object (e.g., a list) as a function default binds it once at definition time, not per call.
: reassigning a name never modifies the old object; forgetting this leads to incorrect update logic.
: Python integers are arbitrary precision and never overflow, but
is a 64-bit IEEE 754 double and does.
— a mapping from string names to object references — for each scope (local, enclosing, global, built-in). When the assignment statement
The right-hand side is evaluated, producing an object.
is entered (or updated) in the current namespace mapping to that object.

previously referred to a different object, that object's reference count decrements. When an object's reference count reaches zero, CPython immediately deallocates it (cyclic garbage is handled separately by the cyclic collector).
The identity of an object — its address in memory in CPython — is returned by
. Two names that refer to the same object satisfy
. Two names that refer to equal but distinct objects satisfy
if its value cannot change after creation.
are all immutable. An object is
if its content can be altered in place:
When a name referring to an immutable object appears on the left-hand side of a compound assignment like
, Python creates a new object for the new value and rebinds the name — it does
modify the original object. This distinction becomes critical when multiple names alias the same object.
: each type implements special methods (dunder methods) that the interpreter calls for operators and built-in functions. For example,
. This lookup chain means any class that implements
can participate in addition regardless of its inheritance tree — the
). This is specified behavior, not a general coercion rule: Python will not implicitly convert

is arbitrary precision: the value 2 raised to the 1000th power is computed exactly. The memory occupied by an integer grows with its magnitude — small integers use more memory per digit than a fixed-width C integer but eliminate overflow entirely.
Floating-point arithmetic follows IEEE 754 binary64. The literal
cannot be represented exactly; its actual stored value is slightly more than 0.1. For currency and other decimal-exact contexts, use
The diagram below shows how names bind to objects, the built-in type hierarchy rooted at
, mutability categories, and common type conversion functions.
The built-in types cover the vast majority of use cases. Choose among them as follows:
: counters, indices, token counts, pagination offsets — any whole number.
: probabilities, similarity scores, embedding coordinates, durations.
: text, API keys, prompts, labels, file paths.
: ordered, mutable sequences — message histories, tool call results, batch items.
: fixed-arity records, return values from functions that return multiple things, dict keys that are composite.
: key-value records — JSON responses, configuration, named parameters, caches.
: fast membership testing, deduplication — seen IDs, disallowed tokens.
: sentinel for optional values and absent results.
When not to use built-in types alone
: when your data has a fixed schema with named fields, prefer a
or a Pydantic model rather than a raw
. When you need decimal exactness, use
. When you need thread-safe queues, use
to inspect it at runtime. Annotate with type hints for static analysis, but the hints do not enforce types at runtime.

# Basic assignments
count: int = 0
score: float = 0.95
label: str = "agent_response"
active: bool = True
data: list = []
config: dict = {}
result = None          # NoneType

# Introspection
print(type(score))          # <class 'float'>
print(isinstance(score, float))  # True
print(isinstance(score, (int, float)))  # True  (checks both)
print(id(count))            # memory address (CPython)

# Type hints don't enforce at runtime
x: int = "oops"    # no error — hints are advisory
All built-in type constructors double as conversion functions. Always handle

# Numeric conversions
print(int("42"))          # 42
print(int(3.9))           # 3  (truncates, does NOT round)
print(float("3.14"))      # 3.14
print(complex(1, 2))      # (1+2j)

# String conversions
print(str(100))            # '100'
print(str(True))           # 'True'
print(str(None))           # 'None'

# Collection conversions
print(list("hello"))       # ['h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o']
print(tuple([1, 2, 3]))    # (1, 2, 3)
print(set([1, 2, 2, 3]))   # {1, 2, 3}
print(dict(a=1, b=2))      # {'a': 1, 'b': 2}

# Boolean truthiness
print(bool(0))       # False
print(bool(""))      # False
print(bool([]))      # False
print(bool(None))    # False
print(bool(1))       # True
print(bool("x"))     # True

# Safe conversion from untrusted input
def safe_int(value: str, default: int = 0) -> int:
    try:
        return int(value)
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        return default

print(safe_int("42"))     # 42
print(safe_int("oops"))   # 0

# ── Aliasing vs copying ───────────────────────────────────────────
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a            # b and a refer to the SAME list object
b.append(4)
print(a)         # [1, 2, 3, 4]  -- a is affected!

c = a.copy()     # shallow copy → new list object
c.append(5)
print(a)         # [1, 2, 3, 4]  -- a is unaffected

# ── Immutable rebinding ───────────────────────────────────────────
x = 10
original_id = id(x)
x += 1           # creates a NEW int object
print(id(x) == original_id)  # False — x now refers to 11

# ── Multiple assignment and unpacking ─────────────────────────────
a, b, c = 1, 2, 3
first, *rest = [10, 20, 30, 40]
print(first, rest)   # 10  [20, 30, 40]

# Swap without a temp variable (tuple packing/unpacking)
x, y = 5, 10
x, y = y, x
print(x, y)      # 10  5

# ── None checks ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
response = None
if response is None:           # correct — identity check
    print("no response yet")
if not response:               # also works, but catches empty string too
    print("falsy")
Example 2: dict and set patterns common in agent code

from typing import Any

# ── Dict: building and accessing structured records ───────────────
tool_call: dict[str, Any] = {
    "name": "search_web",
    "args": {"query": "latest LLM benchmarks", "max_results": 5},
    "call_id": "tc_001",
}

# Safe access with default
query = tool_call.get("args", {}).get("query", "")
print(query)   # latest LLM benchmarks

# Dict update (merging tool metadata)
metadata: dict[str, Any] = {}
metadata.update({"model": "claude-3", "tokens": 1200})
metadata |= {"latency_ms": 430}   # Python 3.9+ merge operator
print(metadata)

# ── Set: deduplication and membership tests ───────────────────────
seen_ids: set[str] = set()
tool_calls = ["tc_001", "tc_002", "tc_001", "tc_003"]

unique = []
for tc_id in tool_calls:
    if tc_id not in seen_ids:   # O(1) average
        seen_ids.add(tc_id)
        unique.append(tc_id)

print(unique)  # ['tc_001', 'tc_002', 'tc_003']

# Set operations
allowed = {"search_web", "run_code", "read_file"}
requested = {"run_code", "delete_db"}
blocked = requested - allowed
print(blocked)  # {'delete_db'}
In a typical agentic pipeline, a language model returns a JSON blob that must be parsed, validated, and dispatched. Each step involves type conversion and type checking.

import json
from typing import Any

RAW_RESPONSE = '''
{
  "tool": "calculator",
  "input": {"expression": "12 * 7"},
  "confidence": "0.98",
  "retries": "2"
}
'''

def parse_tool_response(raw: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Parse, type-coerce, and validate a raw JSON tool response.
    Returns a typed dict ready for dispatch.
    """
    data: dict[str, Any] = json.loads(raw)

    # Validate required keys
    required = {"tool", "input", "confidence"}
    missing = required - data.keys()
    if missing:
        raise ValueError(f"Missing fields: {missing}")

    # Coerce string numerics — LLMs often return numbers as strings
    data["confidence"] = float(data["confidence"])
    data["retries"] = int(data.get("retries", "0"))

    # Validate ranges
    if not (0.0 <= data["confidence"] <= 1.0):
        raise ValueError(f"confidence out of range: {data['confidence']}")

    return data

result = parse_tool_response(RAW_RESPONSE)
print(result["tool"])          # 'calculator'
print(type(result["confidence"]))  # <class 'float'>
print(result["retries"])       # 2  (int, not '2')

# Dispatch: use isinstance for safe branching
tool_name = result["tool"]
assert isinstance(tool_name, str), f"Expected str, got {type(tool_name)}"
print(f"Dispatching tool: {tool_name}")

# WRONG: the default list is created ONCE and reused across calls
def append_item(item, container=[]):
    container.append(item)
    return container

print(append_item(1))  # [1]
print(append_item(2))  # [1, 2]  ← not [2]!

# CORRECT: use None as default, create inside the function
def append_item_fixed(item, container=None):
    if container is None:
        container = []
    container.append(item)
    return container

print(append_item_fixed(1))  # [1]
print(append_item_fixed(2))  # [2]

# WRONG: 'is' tests identity, not equality
a = 1000
b = 1000
print(a is b)   # False (may be True for small ints due to interning, unreliable)
print(a == b)   # True  -- always correct for value comparison

# CORRECT pattern
# Use 'is' only for None, True, False singletons
value = None
if value is None:        # correct
    print("absent")
if value == None:        # works but style guide warns against it
    print("absent")

# int() truncates toward zero
print(int(3.9))   # 3, not 4
print(int(-3.9))  # -3, not -4

# round() for nearest-integer rounding
print(round(3.9))  # 4
print(round(3.5))  # 4  (banker's rounding in Python 3)
print(round(2.5))  # 2  (banker's rounding)

# math.floor / math.ceil for explicit direction
import math
print(math.floor(3.9))   # 3
print(math.ceil(3.1))    # 4
Annotate with type hints on public function signatures; it costs nothing at runtime and enables static checking.
For configuration objects with known schemas, use
When copying a list or dict shallowly, use
. For deep copies of nested structures, use
for integers) — it is an implementation detail of CPython, not the language spec.
in debug messages to see the repr (type-informative) rather than str (type-erasing) representation.
on failure, callers must check; consider raising an exception or returning a typed sentinel instead for better explicitness.
occupies 28 bytes for small values (compared to 4 or 8 bytes for a C integer). A
occupies 24 bytes (C double is 8 bytes). This overhead comes from the object header: reference count, type pointer, and (for

For numerical workloads at scale, use NumPy arrays — they store data in contiguous C-typed memory, bypassing the Python object overhead entirely. A NumPy array of a million 64-bit floats uses 8 MB; a Python list of the same values uses roughly 35 MB (list pointers plus float objects).
CPython pre-allocates integer objects for values in the range
both bind to the same pre-allocated object, so
. Outside this range, each literal creates a new object. This is an optimization detail — never write code that depends on it.
is a hash table; average-case lookups, insertions, and deletions are O(1). As of Python 3.7, insertion order is preserved. Iterating over keys takes O(n). Copying a dict is O(n).
: static type checkers that read PEP 484 annotations and report type mismatches before runtime.
: data validation library that enforces types at runtime using Python annotations; widely used for API schemas in agent frameworks.
— building blocks for rich type signatures.
from annotated class fields; a lightweight alternative to NamedTuple or Pydantic.
: typed numeric arrays with C-level performance; introduces its own scalar types (
) that interoperate with Python's built-in types.
modules: exact decimal or rational arithmetic when IEEE 754 float is not precise enough.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ToolResult:
    name: str
    output: str
    tokens_used: int = 0
    error: Optional[str] = None
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # correct mutable default

tr = ToolResult(name="search", output="Python 3.12 released")
print(tr)
# ToolResult(name='search', output='Python 3.12 released', tokens_used=0, error=None, tags=[])

tr.tags.append("retrieval")
print(tr.tags)   # ['retrieval']
Python's dynamic strong type system sits at a specific point in the design space. The table compares it to nearby languages.
The key differentiator: Python's mutability model is
). Python enforces strong typing at the operation level (no implicit cross-type coercion) while remaining entirely dynamic at the declaration level.
: never assume an API response or user input has the expected type. Always convert and validate before use.
in Python. For equality testing of floats, use
Large integer operations can be slow
: arbitrary-precision arithmetic with very large integers (cryptographic scale) is O(n^2) in digit count for multiplication. Use dedicated libraries (
Dict key types must be hashable
of hashables) can be dict keys. Using a mutable type raises

: if objects reference each other forming a cycle, CPython's reference counter cannot free them; the cyclic garbage collector handles this but adds latency. For long-running agents, avoid accumulating cyclic structures.

Think of Python variables as sticky notes (names) pasted onto physical objects on a table. The note does not contain the object — it points to it. Multiple notes can point to the same object. Peeling a note off and sticking it to a different object (rebinding) does nothing to the original object.

can change. A stone (immutable) can have many notes pointing at it, but the stone never changes — reassigning a note just redirects to a different stone. A whiteboard (mutable) can have many notes pointing at it, and anyone who erases and rewrites the whiteboard changes what

. Every type is a class; every value is an instance. Calling
is not a cast — it calls the constructor of the
class, which creates a new integer object from whatever
A Python variable is a name bound to an object, not a box containing a value. Assignment rebinds the name; it never modifies the existing object.
Mutability is a property of the type, not the name:
only for identity checks against singletons (
Type conversion is explicit in Python. Use the constructor (

to get static checking without runtime overhead.
